title: Information Criteria for Hyper-parameter Tuning
text: implement several information-criterion–based model selection procedures BRIERi.selection, including Bayesian information criterion (BIC), Mallows’ \(C_p\), generalized cross-validation (GCV), select tuning parameter \(\eta\) \(\lambda\). Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). BIC defined \[\begin{equation} \label{eq:BIC} \mathrm{BIC}(\eta,\lambda) =   \frac{1}{n} \left\{   \mathrm{Dev}\!\left(y,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\eta,\lambda}\right)   +     \log(n)\,\mathrm{df}(\eta,\lambda)   \right\}, \end{equation}\] \(\mathrm{Dev}(y,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\eta,\lambda})\) denotes deviance evaluated fitted model tuning parameters \((\eta,\lambda)\), \(\mathrm{df}(\eta,\lambda)\) corresponding effective degrees freedom. Mallows’ \(C_p\). linear models known error variance \(\sigma^2\), Mallows’ \(C_p\) defined \[\begin{equation} \label{eq:Cp} C_p(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}) =   \frac{1}{n} \left\| \boldsymbol{y} - \widehat{\boldsymbol{\mu}} \right\|^2 +   \frac{2\sigma^2}{n}\, \mathrm{df}, \end{equation}\] \(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}\) denotes vector predicted means \(\mathrm{df}\) associated degrees freedom. Generalized Cross-Validation (GCV). Generalized cross-validation provides computationally efficient approximation leave-one-cross-validation given \[\begin{equation} \label{eq:GCV} \mathrm{GCV}(\lambda) =   \frac{\mathrm{Dev}(y,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\lambda})} {n \left\{ 1 - \mathrm{df}(\lambda)/n \right\}^2}. \end{equation}\]

title: Information Criteria for Hyper-parameter Tuning
text: implement several model selection procedures BRIERs.selection summary-level data, including pseudo-validation, Bregman-divergence–based Mallows’ \(C_p\) (\(BRC_p\)), generalized information criteria (GIC), select tuning parameters \(\eta\) \(\lambda\). Pseudo-validation. Originally proposed LASSOsum , pseudo-validation selects tuning parameter \(\lambda\) maximizing objective \[\begin{equation} \label{eq:pseudoval} f(\lambda) =   \frac{\boldsymbol{\beta}_{\lambda}^{\top}\,\widehat{\mathbf{r}}} {\sqrt{\boldsymbol{\beta}_{\lambda}^{\top}\,\widetilde{\boldsymbol{X}}_{0}^{\top}   \widetilde{\boldsymbol{X}}_{0}\,\boldsymbol{\beta}_{\lambda}}}, \end{equation}\] \(\widetilde{\boldsymbol{X}}_{0}\) standardized genotype matrix external reference panel \(\widehat{\mathbf{r}}\) shrinkage-adjusted estimate SNP–phenotype correlation vector. Specifically, element \(\widehat{\mathbf{r}}\) defined \[   \widehat{r}_j   =     r_j\left(1-\mathrm{fdr}_j\right),   \] \(\mathrm{fdr}_j\) denotes local false discovery rate SNP \(j\). objective approximates Spearman correlation predicted genetic score \(\widetilde{\boldsymbol{X}}_{0}\boldsymbol{\beta}_{\lambda}\) unobserved true phenotype. Bregman-divergence based \(C_p\). summary-level settings, adopt Bregman-divergence based extension Mallows’ \(C_p\), referred BRC\(_p\). criterion defined \[\begin{equation} \label{eq:BRCp} \widehat{R}_{\mathrm{BRC}_p} \!\left(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\eta,\lambda}\right) =   D_{\mathcal{E}_{\widetilde{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}}} \!\left(   \widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\eta,\lambda},   \widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}   \right) +   \frac{|\mathcal{S}_{\eta,\lambda}|}{n(1+\eta)} \, D_{\mathcal{E}_{\widetilde{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}}} \!\left(   \widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\eta,\lambda},   \widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}   \right), \end{equation}\] \(D_{\mathcal{E}_{\widetilde{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}}}(\cdot,\cdot)\) denotes Bregman divergence induced quadratic loss respect linkage disequilibrium (LD) matrix \(\widetilde{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}\), \(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) pilot reference coefficient estimate, \(|\mathcal{S}_{\eta,\lambda}|\) size active set associated tuning parameters \((\eta,\lambda)\). factor \(1+\eta\) accounts effective sample-size inflation induced external model integration. Generalized Information Criterion (GIC). implement generalized information criterion summary-level models, defined \[\begin{equation} \label{eq:GIC_s} \mathrm{GIC}(\eta,\lambda) =   \frac{1}{n} \left\{   G\!\left(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\eta,\lambda}\right)   +     a_n\,\mathrm{df}(\eta,\lambda)   \right\}, \end{equation}\] \(G(\cdot)\) denotes goodness--fit measure expressed terms summary statistics, \(\mathrm{df}(\eta,\lambda)\) effective degrees freedom defined previous section, \(a_n\) penalty term controlling model complexity. Throughout work, set \(a_n = \log(n)\), corresponding Bayesian information criterion (BIC). BRIERs, goodness--fit term \(G(\cdot)\) evaluated using Bregman divergence associated quadratic loss induced linkage disequilibrium matrix.

title: Information Criteria for Hyper-parameter Tuning
text: Accurate estimation degrees freedom (DoF) essential information-criterion–based model selection procedures. BRIERi BRIERs can formulated penalized regression problems respect external-model–weighted response, denoted \(\widetilde{\boldsymbol{Y}}\) formulation, effective degrees freedom fitted model can explicitly expressed \[\begin{equation} \label{eq:df_weighted} \mathrm{df} =   \frac{\mathrm{df}(\widetilde{\boldsymbol{Y}})}{1+\eta}, \end{equation}\] \(\mathrm{df}(\widetilde{\boldsymbol{Y}})\) denotes degrees freedom associated weighted response, \(\eta\) integration weight. BRIER2, implement two approaches estimate degrees freedom penalized models. first approach adopts simple computationally efficient estimator uses number active predictors, .e., number nonzero regression coefficients fitted model, estimate degrees freedom. estimator shown consistent LASSO-type problems suitable regularity conditions . second approach implements general degrees--freedom estimator based generalized information criterion (GIC) . Specifically, degrees freedom defined \[\begin{equation} \label{eq:df_gic} \mathrm{df}_{\lambda} \;=\; \mathrm{tr} \left\{   \left[     \nabla^{2}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}}     Q_{\eta, \lambda}\!\left(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\lambda}\right)     \right]^{-1}   \nabla^{2}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}}   \ell\!\left(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\lambda}\right)   \right\}, \end{equation}\] \[   Q_{\eta, \lambda}(\boldsymbol{\beta})   =     Q_{\eta}(\boldsymbol{\beta})   +     p_{\lambda}(\boldsymbol{\beta})   \] denotes penalized objective function penalty function \(p_{\lambda}(\boldsymbol{\beta})\) \(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\lambda}\) estimated coefficient vector tuning parameter \(\lambda\).

text: first introduce search strategy integration weights \(\eta\) using user-specified fixed sequence. default, \(\eta\) grid defined exp{seq(log(0.1), log(10), length = 20)}. BRIERi provides three criterion evaluate candidate pair hyperparameters \((\eta, \lambda)\): cross-validation, evaluation independent validation set, information-based criteria. function BRIERi.cv performs cross-validation training data, using predicted deviance evaluation criteria, specific deviance definition provided Appendix BRIERi.selection can also select optimal hyper-parameters independent validation set provided. displays example using MSPE evaluation metric continuous outcome. code: fit = BRIERi.cv(     X = dat$X,      y = dat$y,      beta.external = dat$beta.external,     eta = exp(seq(0, log(10), length = 20))         family = "gaussian",     penalty = "LASSO" ) X_val = read.table("path/to/file") y_val = read.table("path/to/file") %>% as.vector() out = BRIERi.selection(   fit = fit,   X.val = X_val,    y.val = y_val,   criteria = "gaussian.mspe" )  ## Best eta and lambda based on Cp are reported X_val = read.table("path/to/file") y_val = read.table("path/to/file") %>% as.vector() out = BRIERi.selection(   fit = fit,   criteria = "Cp" )  ## Best eta and lambda based on Cp are reported plot.eta(out)

text: BRIERi.multi requires: individual-level predictor design matrix phenotype data target cohort, one multiple pre-trained external models. External models must provided joint model coefficients (e.g., PRS, penalized regression). GWAS summary statistics marginal predictor–trait inner products available, users instead refer BRIER.S train joint model directly summary-level data. code: library(BRIER2) ## target cohorts X_target = read.table("path/to/file") y_target = read.table("path/to/file") %>% as.vector() names(y_target) == rownames(X_target) length(y_target) == nrow(X_target) # sample name and number of observation should be matched  ## External models beta1 = read.table("path/to/file") %>% as.vector() beta2 = read.table("path/to/file") %>% as.vector() beta3 = read.table("path/to/file") %>% as.vector()  ## reconstruct beta so it matches colnames(X_target) exactly, filling unoverlapped (missing) coefficients with 0 beta_ext1 = setNames(numeric(ncol(X_target)), colnames(X_target)) beta_ext1[intersect(names(beta1), colnames(X_target))] = Ebeta[intersect(names(beta1), colnames(X_target))] beta_ext2 = setNames(numeric(ncol(X_target)), colnames(X_target)) beta_ext2[intersect(names(beta2), colnames(X_target))] = Ebeta[intersect(names(beta2), colnames(X_target))] beta_ext3 = setNames(numeric(ncol(X_target)), colnames(X_target)) beta_ext3[intersect(names(beta3), colnames(X_target))] = Ebeta[intersect(names(beta3), colnames(X_target))] beta_ext = cbind(beta_ext1, beta_ext2, beta_ext3)
